priority: medium
status: PENDING
title: cosmo-smoke v0.8.x — Post-OTel Continuation
---

# cosmo-smoke v0.8.x — Post-OTel Continuation

**Date**: 2026-04-19
**Branch**: master
**Status**: pending

## Current State

cosmo-smoke v0.8.0 with full OpenTelemetry integration. 314 tests passing, 29 assertion types. All 4 OTel depth roadmap items are **complete**:

- ROAD-034: OpenTelemetry trace correlation (v0.8.0)
- ROAD-035: Export smoke results as OTel telemetry (OTLPReporter, MultiReporter)
- ROAD-036: Multi-backend trace verification (Jaeger/Tempo/Honeycomb/Datadog via traceBackend interface)
- ROAD-037: Trace-aware retry (retry_on_trace_only field on RetryPolicy)
- ROAD-038: Watch mode trace health monitoring (TraceHealthTracker sliding window)

The OTel integration is feature-complete for v0.8.x. No pending OTel work.

## Goal

Pivot from OTel depth to strategic breadth. The next phase focuses on two directions:

1. **Ecosystem expansion** — MCP extension for Claude Desktop integration (ROAD-032)
2. **Portfolio visibility** — Dashboard across the ~95-project portfolio (ROAD-039)

Plus backlogged infrastructure items that unlock scale.

## Strategic Priorities (Ordered)

### Priority 1: ROAD-032 — Claude Desktop MCP Extension

**Scope**: Large. Needs its own design session before implementation.

The big strategic pivot. Makes cosmo-smoke usable from Claude Desktop via MCP protocol. Users can ask Claude to generate, run, and debug smoke tests conversationally.

**What to figure out first**:
- MCP tool definitions: which smoke operations to expose (run, init, validate, list-tests)
- How to handle long-running test execution in MCP context (streaming vs polling)
- Config discovery: how Claude Desktop finds `.smoke.yaml` files in a project
- Error surfacing: assertion failures formatted for conversational debugging
- Whether this is a standalone MCP server binary or a mode of the existing `smoke` binary

**Recommended approach**: Design session first (brainstorm + plan), then implement as `smoke mcp` subcommand or separate binary.

### Priority 2: ROAD-039 — Portfolio Smoke Dashboard

**Scope**: Medium-large.

Dashboard that aggregates `smoke run --format json` results across the ~95-project CosmoLabs portfolio. Uses `smoke serve` endpoint to provide real-time status.

**Key design questions**:
- Backend: in-process HTTP server in `smoke serve` or separate dashboard binary
- Data collection: pull model (dashboard fetches from each project) vs push (projects report to dashboard)
- Storage: in-memory ring buffer vs persistent store
- Auth: portfolio-wide API key or per-project tokens

### Priority 3: ROAD-010 — Monorepo Sub-Config Support

**Scope**: Medium. Has existing plan.

`--monorepo` flag auto-discovers `.smoke.yaml` in subdirectories. Unlimited depth, configurable exclusions. Already has architectural design in place.

**Carry-over**: Check `docs/planning-mode/` for existing plan file.

### Priority 4: ROAD-033 — Parallel-Agent Merge-Conflict SOP

**Scope**: Small. Process improvement.

SOP for handling merge conflicts when multiple GLM agents modify overlapping files. Documentation-only.

## Open Ideas

- **IDEA-MO1FC22M**: Mobile app deep link assertion — seed status, recently updated with multi-project-type scope (Android/iOS/React Native). Worth exploring if mobile testing becomes a portfolio need.

## Carry-Overs

### Technical Context
- 314 tests, all passing. Test suite is healthy.
- Build: `go build ./...` — clean. Binary at `./smoke`.
- OTel code lives in `internal/runner/` (reporter, trace backend, retry integration).
- Reporter interface in `internal/reporter/` supports pluggable formats.
- Config schema in `internal/schema/` — all assertion types are pure functions.

### Architecture Notes for MCP (ROAD-032)
- `cmd/` uses Cobra — new subcommand follows established pattern
- Reporter interface already abstracts output format — MCP can add a `MCPReporter`
- `internal/schema/` has YAML parsing + validation that MCP tools can reuse
- Existing `smoke init` already does project detection + template generation — MCP tool can call same logic
- No external HTTP server dependency currently — `smoke serve` for dashboard would be new territory

## Session Entry Recommendations

1. Start with `/triage` to get full work source scan
2. For ROAD-032: run `/brainplan "Claude Desktop MCP extension for cosmo-smoke"` — this needs design before code
3. For ROAD-039: check if any brainstorming docs already exist in `docs/ideas/`
4. Commit any uncommitted changes from this session before starting new work
5. Consider bumping version to v0.9.0-dev after committing the OTel changelog entries

## Reference Files

- `CLAUDE.md` — project architecture, assertion types, build/test commands
- `docs/roadmap/index.yaml` — full roadmap with status
- `docs/ideas/2026-04-16-portfolio-smoke-dashboard.md` — dashboard idea
- `docs/ideas/2026-04-16-graphql-introspection-assertion.md` — recently implemented
- `internal/runner/` — assertion engine, OTel reporter, trace backends
- `internal/reporter/` — terminal/JSON/JUnit/TAP/Prometheus reporters
- `internal/schema/` — config structs and validation

## What Got Done (Amendment — 2026-04-19)

**Session type**: Cleanup and session-end. No new feature work started.

**Commits made**: 5 commits on master, all uncommitted changes from previous session were committed:
- `b2bef56` chore: session-end documentation
- `fd6d4ab` chore: update GOrchestra intel metadata
- `7bb8e58` docs: session-end documentation and metadata updates
- `4ba01d8` docs: add USAGE.md to all 11 CCS-managed directories
- `0e8f984` chore: upgrade project to CCS standards

**Working tree**: Clean after commits. No uncommitted changes remaining.

**Strategic priorities**: Unchanged. None of these were touched:
- ROAD-032 (MCP extension) — still priority 1, still needs design session
- ROAD-039 (Portfolio dashboard) — still priority 2
- ROAD-010 (Monorepo sub-config) — still priority 3

**Version**: Still v0.8.0. No version bump performed. The changelog entries for OTel features remain in `docs/changelog/unreleased.yaml` — these should be released before bumping to v0.9.0-dev.

**Status**: Still `pending` — no strategic roadmap work was completed this session.

content.text:
✅ **ROAD-010 is already implemented.** The monorepo sub-config support was completed as part of v0.7 — discovery, runner integration, `--monorepo` flag, exclusions, all wired up. Updated roadmap status to `completed`.

Remaining priorities:
- **ROAD-032** — MCP extension (design needed)
- **ROAD-039** — Portfolio dashboard (check existing ideas)

Want to tackle one of these?

# /workcheck — Holistic Session Verification

Answers: **"Did I finish what I started? What did I miss?"**

Goes beyond prompt goals — checks TaskList, detects stale metadata, finds drift, scans structural gaps (including roadmap), and generates actionable suggestions.

## Usage

```bash
/workcheck              # Full holistic verification
```

## Execution

### Step 1: Gather Evidence (parallel)

Run simultaneously:
1. `ccs workcheck --json` via Bash — returns goals, commits, files, doc gaps, tasks, stale metadata, drift, gaps, suggestions
2. Call TaskList to get current in-memory task state

### Step 2: Persist TaskList

Write current TaskList to `.claude/task-log.jsonl` (append, one JSON per line):
```json
{"id":"N","subject":"...","status":"...","created_at":"...","session":"current"}
```
This survives context compaction and feeds future workcheck runs.

### Step 3: Read Plan Sources

From CCS JSON, read `prompt_path`, `brainstorm_ref`, `plan_ref`. Skip missing refs. Use these for goal-commit matching context.

### Step 4: Verify Goals

Match each goal against commits + files changed. Classify:

| Status | Criteria |
|--------|----------|
| DONE | Commit message matches goal, relevant files changed |
| PARTIAL | Some files changed but goal not fully addressed |
| NOT STARTED | No matching commits or file changes |
| BLOCKED | Dependencies incomplete |

### Step 5: Verify Tasks

Cross-reference TaskList against commits. Flag:
- Tasks marked `completed` without evidence (no matching commit)
- Tasks still `pending`/`in_progress` that should be done
- Tasks not in TaskList but present in plan (missed creation)

### Step 6: Check Stale Metadata

Review `stale_metadata` from CCS JSON. For clear cases, auto-fix:
- `ccs feedback done FB-XXX` for feedback with modified affected files
- `ccs issues update BUG-XXX --status closed` for fixed issues

Report fixes applied and items needing manual review.

### Step 7: Detect Drift

Review `drift` from CCS JSON. Classify using conversation context:
- **user-requested**: Unplanned but explicitly asked by user — not a problem
- **truly unplanned**: Work that happened without being tracked — note for awareness
- **metadata/merge**: Expected overhead — ignore

### Step 8: Scan Gaps (including Roadmap)

Review `gaps` from CCS JSON. Categorize and act:

| Gap Type | Severity | Action |
|----------|----------|--------|
| `missing_test` | critical | Flag — should fix this session |
| `missing_symlink` | advisory | Note for next session |
| `misclassified_commit` | advisory | Note for awareness |
| `missing_roadmap` | advisory | Offer to add/update roadmap entry |
| `broken_ref` | critical | Flag — fix reference |

For roadmap gaps: offer to run `ccs roadmap add "title"` or `ccs roadmap update ROAD-XXX --status completed`.

### Step 9: Output Report

```
## Workcheck Report

### Goals: N/M complete
| # | Goal | Status | Evidence |
...

### Tasks: N/M complete
| ID | Task | Status | Notes |
...

### Stale Metadata (N items)
- [auto-fixed] FB-XXX: status updated
- [needs review] BUG-XXX: affected files changed

### Drift (N commits)
- abc1234: "unplanned feature" — [unplanned]

### Gaps (N found)
- [critical] missing_test: cmd/newcmd.go
- [advisory] missing_roadmap: feat commit without entry

### Suggestions
**This session:**
- Fix critical gaps
- Update stale metadata

**Next session:**
- Address advisory items
- Continue deferred goals

### Forward Intelligence
[Offer to update continuation prompt with remaining/deferred work]
```

## Related

`/remind-me` | `/sweep` | `/what-now` | `/triage` | `/session-end`

content.content:
Roadmap tracking for CCS project direction.

Items are stored in docs/roadmap/index.yaml and docs/roadmap/items/*.yaml.

LISTING:
  ccs roadmap                         List active roadmap items
  ccs roadmap --all                   Include promoted/archived
  ccs roadmap --status exploring      Filter by status
  ccs roadmap --category core         Filter by category
  ccs roadmap --area glm-queue        Filter by area tag (partial match)
  ccs roadmap --compact               Compact output

CRUD:
  ccs roadmap add "title"             Capture a new idea
  ccs roadmap add "title" --ref path  Capture with linked reference
  ccs roadmap get ROAD-001            Show item details
  ccs roadmap update ROAD-001 ...     Update fields
  ccs roadmap link ROAD-001 path      Link a reference
  ccs roadmap search "keyword"        Full-text search items

HIERARCHY:
  ccs roadmap areas                   Show area tags with completion rates
  ccs roadmap tree                    Show parent-child hierarchy
  ccs roadmap tree --area glm         Filter tree by area tag
  ccs roadmap update ID --parent X    Set parent relationship
  ccs roadmap update ID --add-tag X   Append a tag (e.g., "area:glm-queue")

GROOMING:
  ccs roadmap groom ROAD-001          Show what needs enrichment
  ccs roadmap triage                  Walk captured items needing grooming

LIFECYCLE:
  ccs roadmap promote ROAD-001        Convert to FEAT/TASK issue

INTELLIGENCE:
  ccs roadmap next                    Highest-value planned item
  ccs roadmap quick-wins              Small items with plans
  ccs roadmap analyze                 GLM strategic analysis
  ccs roadmap --smart                 GLM-enhanced listing

input.content:
# Parallel-Agent Merge-Conflict SOP

Handling merge conflicts when multiple GLM agents modify overlapping files during GOrchestra execution.

## Detection

After `ccs merge --all`, conflicts surface as:

```
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in internal/schema/schema.go
Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and commit the result.
```

`ccs verify-worktree --all` also flags conflicting files pre-merge.

## Resolution Strategies

### Strategy 1: Sequential Re-merge

When conflict is minor (adjacent additions, not overlapping edits):

1. `git mergetool` or manual edit to resolve
2. `git add <files> && git commit`
3. Re-run `ccs verify-worktree` on the resolved merge

### Strategy 2: Rebase Agent onto Master

When agent A's work depends on agent B's output:

1. Merge agent B first: `ccs merge <agent-b>`
2. Rebase agent A: `git -C <worktree-a-dir> rebase master`
3. Resolve conflicts in worktree context
4. `ccs verify-worktree <agent-a> && ccs merge <agent-a>`

### Strategy 3: Cherry-Pick

When one agent's changes to a file are more complete:

1. Merge the more complete agent first
2. Cherry-pick specific commits from the other: `git cherry-pick <sha>`
3. Resolve conflicts per commit

## Prevention

### File Partitioning

The primary prevention strategy. In the plan phase, assign agents to non-overlapping files:

- Agent A: `internal/schema/` + `cmd/`
- Agent B: `internal/runner/` + `internal/reporter/`
- Agent C: `docs/` + `tests for A+B`

### Interface Contracts

When agents must share types, define the interface in the plan:

```go
// Both agents use this type — defined in plan, implemented once
type TraceResult struct {
    TraceID  string
    SpanCount int
}
```

Agent who "owns" the defining file implements it. Other agents depend on the interface.

### Shared-Stub Pattern

For new types both agents need:

1. Agent A creates a minimal stub in a shared file
2. Agent B works against the stub
3. Post-merge, the full implementation replaces the stub

### Tag-Based Isolation

Use Go build tags for truly independent implementations:

```go
// agent_a.go
//go:build agentA

// agent_b.go
//go:build !agentA
```

## Escalation

If resolution requires architectural decisions not in the plan:

1. Abort the conflicting merge: `git merge --abort`
2. Document the conflict: `ccs feedback send . "merge conflict between agents X and Y on file Z"`
3. Resolve in a focused session with full context

## Checklist

- [ ] Review plan for file overlap before dispatching agents
- [ ] Run `ccs verify-worktree --all` before batch merge
- [ ] Merge agents with shared files last
- [ ] Test after every merge resolution

toolUseResult.content:
# Parallel-Agent Merge-Conflict SOP

Handling merge conflicts when multiple GLM agents modify overlapping files during GOrchestra execution.

## Detection

After `ccs merge --all`, conflicts surface as:

```
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in internal/schema/schema.go
Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and commit the result.
```

`ccs verify-worktree --all` also flags conflicting files pre-merge.

## Resolution Strategies

### Strategy 1: Sequential Re-merge

When conflict is minor (adjacent additions, not overlapping edits):

1. `git mergetool` or manual edit to resolve
2. `git add <files> && git commit`
3. Re-run `ccs verify-worktree` on the resolved merge

### Strategy 2: Rebase Agent onto Master

When agent A's work depends on agent B's output:

1. Merge agent B first: `ccs merge <agent-b>`
2. Rebase agent A: `git -C <worktree-a-dir> rebase master`
3. Resolve conflicts in worktree context
4. `ccs verify-worktree <agent-a> && ccs merge <agent-a>`

### Strategy 3: Cherry-Pick

When one agent's changes to a file are more complete:

1. Merge the more complete agent first
2. Cherry-pick specific commits from the other: `git cherry-pick <sha>`
3. Resolve conflicts per commit

## Prevention

### File Partitioning

The primary prevention strategy. In the plan phase, assign agents to non-overlapping files:

- Agent A: `internal/schema/` + `cmd/`
- Agent B: `internal/runner/` + `internal/reporter/`
- Agent C: `docs/` + `tests for A+B`

### Interface Contracts

When agents must share types, define the interface in the plan:

```go
// Both agents use this type — defined in plan, implemented once
type TraceResult struct {
    TraceID  string
    SpanCount int
}
```

Agent who "owns" the defining file implements it. Other agents depend on the interface.

### Shared-Stub Pattern

For new types both agents need:

1. Agent A creates a minimal stub in a shared file
2. Agent B works against the stub
3. Post-merge, the full implementation replaces the stub

### Tag-Based Isolation

Use Go build tags for truly independent implementations:

```go
// agent_a.go
//go:build agentA

// agent_b.go
//go:build !agentA
```

## Escalation

If resolution requires architectural decisions not in the plan:

1. Abort the conflicting merge: `git merge --abort`
2. Document the conflict: `ccs feedback send . "merge conflict between agents X and Y on file Z"`
3. Resolve in a focused session with full context

## Checklist

- [ ] Review plan for file overlap before dispatching agents
- [ ] Run `ccs verify-worktree --all` before batch merge
- [ ] Merge agents with shared files last
- [ ] Test after every merge resolution

input.prompt:
You are brainstorming the design for ROAD-032: Claude Desktop MCP extension for cosmo-smoke.

Context: cosmo-smoke is a Go smoke test runner. It reads `.smoke.yaml` configs and runs lightweight smoke tests (29 assertion types, 314 tests). Architecture: cmd/ (Cobra CLI), internal/schema/ (YAML parsing+validation), internal/runner/ (assertion engine), internal/reporter/ (pluggable output formats), internal/monorepo/ (sub-config discovery), internal/detector/ (project type detection).

The goal: Make cosmo-smoke usable from Claude Desktop via MCP protocol. Users ask Claude to generate, run, and debug smoke tests conversationally.

Research the MCP (Model Context Protocol) spec and design an extension. Write the brainstorm to docs/brainstorming/2026-04-19-claude-desktop-mcp-extension.md covering:

1. **MCP Tool Definitions**: Which operations to expose:
   - `smoke_run` — run tests, return structured results
   - `smoke_init` — generate .smoke.yaml for a project (reuse existing detector logic)
   - `smoke_validate` — validate a .smoke.yaml config
   - `smoke_list` — list available test configs and their assertions
   - Any others that make sense

2. **Architecture Decision**: Standalone `smoke-mcp` binary vs `smoke mcp` subcommand
   - Pros/cons of each approach
   - Recommend one with justification

3. **Long-Running Tests**: How to handle test execution that takes >30s in MCP context
   - Streaming responses vs polling pattern
   - Progress indicators

4. **Config Discovery**: How Claude Desktop finds .smoke.yaml files
   - Working directory detection
   - Monorepo support

5. **Error Surfacing**: How to format assertion failures for conversational debugging
   - Structured error format
   - Suggestions for fixes

6. **Dependencies**: What Go MCP libraries exist? Check for `github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go` or similar.

7. **Implementation Plan**: Rough file structure and implementation order

Keep it technical and actionable. This brainstorm feeds directly into a planning session.

You are brainstorming the design for ROAD-039: Portfolio Smoke Dashboard for cosmo-smoke.

Context: cosmo-smoke is a Go smoke test runner used across CosmoLabs' ~95-project portfolio. It outputs results in multiple formats (terminal, JSON, JUnit, TAP, Prometheus). There's already a `smoke serve` command that runs an HTTP health endpoint. Architecture: cmd/ (Cobra CLI), internal/runner/ (assertion engine), internal/reporter/ (pluggable formats including JSON).

The goal: Dashboard that aggregates `smoke run --format json` results across the entire portfolio for real-time visibility into project health.

First, read the existing idea doc at docs/ideas/2026-04-16-portfolio-smoke-dashboard.md to see what's already been considered.

Then write the brainstorm to docs/brainstorming/2026-04-19-portfolio-smoke-dashboard.md covering:

1. **Backend Architecture**: In-process HTTP server in `smoke serve` vs separate binary
   - `smoke serve` already exists but is minimal (health endpoint only)
   - Extend it or build separate `smoke-dashboard` binary?
   - Recommend one with justification

2. **Data Collection Model**: 
   - Pull model: dashboard fetches from each project's `smoke serve` endpoint
   - Push model: projects report to central dashboard via `smoke run --report-url`
   - Hybrid approach
   - Recommend one with justification for a 95-project portfolio

3. **Storage**: 
   - In-memory ring buffer (last N runs per project)
   - SQLite for persistent history
   - Recommend based on operational simplicity

4. **Frontend**: 
   - Embedded static HTML+JS (no build step, single binary)
   - Consider: vanilla JS with fetch API, or lightweight templating
   - Must work without npm/node

5. **API Design**: 
   - `GET /api/projects` — list all projects with latest status
   - `GET /api/projects/{name}/history` — run history
   - `POST /api/results` — push endpoint for projects
   - `GET /dashboard` — HTML dashboard

6. **Auth**: Portfolio-wide API key vs per-project tokens vs none for internal network

7. **Implementation Plan**: Rough file structure and implementation order

Keep it technical and actionable. Prefer operational simplicity — this runs on internal infra, not public-facing.

lastPrompt: Explore roadmap and let's start working on more eatures

<output-file>/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-gab-PROJECTS-cosmo-smoke/27e7045f-407c-4923-97c7-74ab8ddf7c49/tasks/ab5a235f4b1dbc7d8.output</output-file>
<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Brainstorm ROAD-039 dashboard" completed</summary>
<result>The brainstorm document has been written to `/Users/gab/PROJECTS/cosmo-smoke/docs/brainstorming/2026-04-19-portfolio-smoke-dashboard.md`.

Summary of the design decisions:

1. **Backend**: Extend `smoke serve` with a `--dashboard` flag. No separate binary. The existing HTTP scaffolding, config loading, and graceful shutdown are all reusable. Without `--dashboard`, behavior is identical to today.

2. **Data collection**: Push model. Projects already run `smoke run` in CI -- adding `--report-url` is a one-line change per pipeline. Push avoids the need for service discovery across 95 projects and matches the existing execution model (run-and-exit, not long-lived agent).

3. **Storage**: SQLite via `modernc.org/sqlite` (pure Go, no CGO). Handles the append-only write pattern (~2000 writes/day) with no external database server. Falls back to in-memory for testing. One new dependency.

4. **Frontend**: Single `index.html` embedded via `embed.FS`. Vanilla JS with `fetch()` API, auto-refresh every 30s. No npm, no build step, no framework. Dark terminal aesthetic matching cosmo-smoke's Lipgloss output.

5. **API**: Four routes (`POST /api/results`, `GET /api/projects`, `GET /api/projects/{name}/history`, `GET /dashboard`). The push payload is the same JSON that `--format json` already produces.

6. **Auth**: Single shared API key on `POST /api/results`, unauthenticated reads on internal network. Key rotation is a one-place change (shared CI secret).

7. **Implementation**: ~1040 lines across 5 phases, starting with the push reporter (smallest surface area, immediately useful even without the dashboard server).</result>

<result>The brainstorm document has been written to `/Users/gab/PROJECTS/cosmo-smoke/docs/brainstorming/2026-04-19-claude-desktop-mcp-extension.md`.

**Summary of what was designed:**

**Seven MCP tools** covering the full lifecycle:
1. `smoke_run` -- execute tests, returns structured JSON results with fix suggestions
2. `smoke_init` -- generate .smoke.yaml from project detection or container inspection
3. `smoke_validate` -- validate config without running tests
4. `smoke_list` -- enumerate tests, tags, and assertion types
5. `smoke_explain` -- look up assertion type documentation (prevents hallucination)
6. `smoke_discover` -- find .smoke.yaml files in a directory tree
7. `smoke_generate_test` -- generate a single test YAML snippet

**Key architectural decisions:**
- **`smoke mcp` subcommand** (not standalone binary) -- single binary, simpler distribution, follows the `smoke serve` precedent
- **`mark3labs/mcp-go` v0.34+** as the only new dependency -- implements MCP spec 2025-11-25, supports task-augmented tools, stdio transport, progress notifications
- **Task-augmented `smoke_run`** via `TaskSupportOptional` -- fast tests return synchronously, slow tests return a task ID for polling with progress notifications
- **Stateless design** -- each tool call is self-contained, no session state needed, config path resolved via explicit parameter or walk-up from cwd
- **Fix suggestion engine** -- maps assertion failure patterns to concrete remediation actions (e.g., "Redis not running" -> "docker run -d -p 6379:6379 redis:alpine")

**Implementation estimate**: ~1350 LOC across 13 new files, ~8.5 hours of work, phased in 6 stages from core server through polish.</result>

1	# Portfolio Smoke Dashboard (ROAD-039)
2	
3	**Date**: 2026-04-19
4	**Status**: Brainstorm
5	**Source**: IDEA-MO1FBRNZ, promoted to ROAD-039
6	
7	## Goal
8	
9	Central dashboard that aggregates `smoke run` results across CosmoLabs' ~95 projects for real-time portfolio health visibility. Single binary, zero external deps, runs on internal infra.
10	
11	---
12	
13	## 1. Backend Architecture: Extend `smoke serve`
14	
15	**Recommendation: Extend `smoke serve` in-place. No separate binary.**
16	
17	Why not a separate binary:
18	- The `smoke serve` command already has HTTP scaffolding (Cobra registration, `http.Server`, graceful shutdown, config loading). It is 158 lines and does one thing: run tests on `/healthz` and return JSON. Extending it is straightforward.
19	- A separate `smoke-dashboard` binary means a second build target, a second release pipeline, and two binaries to deploy. For internal tooling across 95 projects, one binary is simpler.
20	- The dashboard is fundamentally "serve HTTP with smoke data" -- it is the same domain as `smoke serve`.
21	
22	How `smoke serve` grows:
23	
24	```
25	smoke serve --port 8080 --dashboard
26	```
27	
28	The `--dashboard` flag activates the aggregation mode. Without it, `smoke serve` behaves exactly as today (health-only). With it, the server gains these routes:
29	
30	| Route | Purpose |
31	|-------|---------|
32	| `GET /healthz` | Existing behavior, unchanged |
33	| `POST /api/results` | Push endpoint for project reporters |
34	| `GET /api/projects` | List all projects with latest status |
35	| `GET /api/projects/{name}/history` | Run history for a project |
36	| `GET /dashboard` | HTML dashboard (embedded) |
37	
38	The health-only mode keeps working for projects that just need a kube probe. The dashboard mode is opt-in.
39	
40	---
41	
42	## 2. Data Collection Model: Push
43	
44	**Recommendation: Push model via `--report-url` flag on `smoke run`.**
45	
46	Why push over pull for 95 projects:
47	
48	- **Pull requires service discovery.** The dashboard would need to know the address of every project's `smoke serve` endpoint. That means a config file listing 95 URLs, or DNS-based discovery, or a service registry. All operational overhead.
49	- **Projects already run `smoke run` in CI.** Every CosmoLabs project runs smoke tests in CI pipelines. Adding `--report-url https://dashboard.internal:8080/api/results` to those CI jobs is a one-line change. The data flows exactly where it needs to go.
50	- **No agent required.** Pull means each project must run `smoke serve` as a long-lived process. Most projects don't -- they run `smoke run` and exit. Push matches the existing execution model.
51	- **CI is the natural source of truth.** Smoke results come from CI. CI pushes them to the dashboard. No additional infrastructure per project.
52	
53	Implementation:
54	
55	```bash
56	# In CI (each project's pipeline)
57	smoke run --format json --report-url https://smoke-dashboard.internal:8080/api/results
58	```
59	
60	The `--report-url` flag causes `smoke run` to POST its JSON output to the URL after the run completes. This is a fire-and-forget HTTP POST in the existing `Summary()` path, identical to how `OTelReporter` already exports telemetry. The payload is exactly the same JSON that `--format json` produces today (`jsonOutput` struct).

file.content:
# Portfolio Smoke Dashboard (ROAD-039)

**Date**: 2026-04-19
**Status**: Brainstorm
**Source**: IDEA-MO1FBRNZ, promoted to ROAD-039

## Goal

Central dashboard that aggregates `smoke run` results across CosmoLabs' ~95 projects for real-time portfolio health visibility. Single binary, zero external deps, runs on internal infra.

---

## 1. Backend Architecture: Extend `smoke serve`

**Recommendation: Extend `smoke serve` in-place. No separate binary.**

Why not a separate binary:
- The `smoke serve` command already has HTTP scaffolding (Cobra registration, `http.Server`, graceful shutdown, config loading). It is 158 lines and does one thing: run tests on `/healthz` and return JSON. Extending it is straightforward.
- A separate `smoke-dashboard` binary means a second build target, a second release pipeline, and two binaries to deploy. For internal tooling across 95 projects, one binary is simpler.
- The dashboard is fundamentally "serve HTTP with smoke data" -- it is the same domain as `smoke serve`.

How `smoke serve` grows:

```
smoke serve --port 8080 --dashboard
```

The `--dashboard` flag activates the aggregation mode. Without it, `smoke serve` behaves exactly as today (health-only). With it, the server gains these routes:

| Route | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `GET /healthz` | Existing behavior, unchanged |
| `POST /api/results` | Push endpoint for project reporters |
| `GET /api/projects` | List all projects with latest status |
| `GET /api/projects/{name}/history` | Run history for a project |
| `GET /dashboard` | HTML dashboard (embedded) |

The health-only mode keeps working for projects that just need a kube probe. The dashboard mode is opt-in.

---

## 2. Data Collection Model: Push

**Recommendation: Push model via `--report-url` flag on `smoke run`.**

Why push over pull for 95 projects:

- **Pull requires service discovery.** The dashboard would need to know the address of every project's `smoke serve` endpoint. That means a config file listing 95 URLs, or DNS-based discovery, or a service registry. All operational overhead.
- **Projects already run `smoke run` in CI.** Every CosmoLabs project runs smoke tests in CI pipelines. Adding `--report-url https://dashboard.internal:8080/api/results` to those CI jobs is a one-line change. The data flows exactly where it needs to go.
- **No agent required.** Pull means each project must run `smoke serve` as a long-lived process. Most projects don't -- they run `smoke run` and exit. Push matches the existing execution model.
- **CI is the natural source of truth.** Smoke results come from CI. CI pushes them to the dashboard. No additional infrastructure per project.

Implementation:

```bash
# In CI (each project's pipeline)
smoke run --format json --report-url https://smoke-dashboard.internal:8080/api/results
```

The `--report-url` flag causes `smoke run` to POST its JSON output to the URL after the run completes. This is a fire-and-forget HTTP POST in the existing `Summary()` path, identical to how `OTelReporter` already exports telemetry. The payload is exactly the same JSON that `--format json` produces today (`jsonOutput` struct).

1	# Claude Desktop MCP Extension for cosmo-smoke
2	
3	**Date**: 2026-04-19
4	**Roadmap**: ROAD-032
5	**Status**: Brainstorm
6	
7	## Goal
8	
9	Make cosmo-smoke usable from Claude Desktop (and any MCP client) via the Model Context Protocol. Users ask Claude to generate, run, and debug smoke tests conversationally -- no CLI knowledge required.
10	
11	---
12	
13	## 1. MCP Tool Definitions
14	
15	Seven tools covering the full smoke test lifecycle. Each maps to existing internal packages with minimal new logic.
16	
17	### 1.1 `smoke_run` -- Execute smoke tests
18	
19	**Purpose**: Run tests and return structured results for conversational debugging.
20	
21	```json
22	{
23	  "name": "smoke_run",
24	  "description": "Run smoke tests from a .smoke.yaml config file. Returns pass/fail results with assertion details for each test. Use this to verify services are healthy, check endpoints, validate configs, or debug failures.",
25	  "inputSchema": {
26	    "type": "object",
27	    "properties": {
28	      "config_path": {
29	        "type": "string",
30	        "description": "Path to .smoke.yaml (default: .smoke.yaml in working directory)"
31	      },
32	      "tags": {
33	        "type": "array",
34	        "items": { "type": "string" },
35	        "description": "Include only tests with these tags"
36	      },
37	      "exclude_tags": {
38	        "type": "array",
39	        "items": { "type": "string" },
40	        "description": "Exclude tests with these tags"
41	      },
42	      "fail_fast": {
43	        "type": "boolean",
44	        "description": "Stop on first failure (default: false)"
45	      },
46	      "timeout": {
47	        "type": "string",
48	        "description": "Per-test timeout override, e.g. '30s'"
49	      },
50	      "dry_run": {
51	        "type": "boolean",
52	        "description": "List tests without running them (default: false)"
53	      },
54	      "monorepo": {
55	        "type": "boolean",
56	        "description": "Discover and run .smoke.yaml in subdirectories (default: false)"
57	      },
58	      "env": {
59	        "type": "string",
60	        "description": "Load environment-specific config (e.g. 'staging' loads staging.smoke.yaml)"

1	# Portfolio Smoke Dashboard (ROAD-039)
2	
3	**Date**: 2026-04-19
4	**Status**: Brainstorm
5	**Source**: IDEA-MO1FBRNZ, promoted to ROAD-039
6	
7	## Goal
8	
9	Central dashboard that aggregates `smoke run` results across CosmoLabs' ~95 projects for real-time portfolio health visibility. Single binary, zero external deps, runs on internal infra.
10	
11	---
12	
13	## 1. Backend Architecture: Extend `smoke serve`
14	
15	**Recommendation: Extend `smoke serve` in-place. No separate binary.**
16	
17	Why not a separate binary:
18	- The `smoke serve` command already has HTTP scaffolding (Cobra registration, `http.Server`, graceful shutdown, config loading). It is 158 lines and does one thing: run tests on `/healthz` and return JSON. Extending it is straightforward.
19	- A separate `smoke-dashboard` binary means a second build target, a second release pipeline, and two binaries to deploy. For internal tooling across 95 projects, one binary is simpler.
20	- The dashboard is fundamentally "serve HTTP with smoke data" -- it is the same domain as `smoke serve`.
21	
22	How `smoke serve` grows:
23	
24	```
25	smoke serve --port 8080 --dashboard
26	```
27	
28	The `--dashboard` flag activates the aggregation mode. Without it, `smoke serve` behaves exactly as today (health-only). With it, the server gains these routes:
29	
30	| Route | Purpose |
31	|-------|---------|
32	| `GET /healthz` | Existing behavior, unchanged |
33	| `POST /api/results` | Push endpoint for project reporters |
34	| `GET /api/projects` | List all projects with latest status |
35	| `GET /api/projects/{name}/history` | Run history for a project |
36	| `GET /dashboard` | HTML dashboard (embedded) |
37	
38	The health-only mode keeps working for projects that just need a kube probe. The dashboard mode is opt-in.
39	
40	---
41	
42	## 2. Data Collection Model: Push
43	
44	**Recommendation: Push model via `--report-url` flag on `smoke run`.**
45	
46	Why push over pull for 95 projects:
47	
48	- **Pull requires service discovery.** The dashboard would need to know the address of every project's `smoke serve` endpoint. That means a config file listing 95 URLs, or DNS-based discovery, or a service registry. All operational overhead.
49	- **Projects already run `smoke run` in CI.** Every CosmoLabs project runs smoke tests in CI pipelines. Adding `--report-url https://dashboard.internal:8080/api/results` to those CI jobs is a one-line change. The data flows exactly where it needs to go.
50	- **No agent required.** Pull means each project must run `smoke serve` as a long-lived process. Most projects don't -- they run `smoke run` and exit. Push matches the existing execution model.
51	- **CI is the natural source of truth.** Smoke results come from CI. CI pushes them to the dashboard. No additional infrastructure per project.
52	
53	Implementation:
54	
55	```bash
56	# In CI (each project's pipeline)
57	smoke run --format json --report-url https://smoke-dashboard.internal:8080/api/results
58	```
59	
60	The `--report-url` flag causes `smoke run` to POST its JSON output to the URL after the run completes. This is a fire-and-forget HTTP POST in the existing `Summary()` path, identical to how `OTelReporter` already exports telemetry. The payload is exactly the same JSON that `--format json` produces today (`jsonOutput` struct).
61	
62	Add a `PushReporter` alongside `OTelReporter` in `internal/reporter/`:
63	
64	```go
65	type PushReporter struct {
66	    endpoint  string
67	    headers   map[string]string
68	    client    *http.Client
69	    // accumulates results, POSTs on Summary()
70	}
71	```
72	
73	The `--report-url` flag wires it in via `withPushReport()` in `cmd/run.go`, same pattern as `withOTelExport()`.
74	
75	---
76	
77	## 3. Storage: SQLite
78	
79	**Recommendation: SQLite via `modernc.org/sqlite` (pure Go, no CGO).**
80	
81	Why not in-memory ring buffer:
82	- A ring buffer loses all data on restart. For a dashboard tracking portfolio health, historical trends matter. "Last 24 hours of CI" is the minimum useful window.
83	- Ring buffers also require manual compaction logic. SQLite handles this natively.
84	
85	Why SQLite over Postgres/MySQL:
86	- Single binary deployment. No database server to run, connect, or configure.
87	- The write pattern is append-only (one POST per CI run, ~95 projects, maybe 10-20 runs/day each = ~2000 writes/day). SQLite handles this trivially.
88	- Read pattern is simple range queries (last N runs for project X). SQLite is excellent at this.
89	- `modernc.org/sqlite` is pure Go -- no CGO, cross-compile works, matches cosmo-smoke's "minimal deps" philosophy.
90	
91	Schema:
92	
93	```sql
94	CREATE TABLE runs (
95	    id          INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
96	    project     TEXT NOT NULL,
97	    timestamp   DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
98	    total       INTEGER,
99	    passed      INTEGER,
100	    failed      INTEGER,
101	    skipped     INTEGER,
102	    allowed_failures INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
103	    duration_ms INTEGER,
104	    payload     TEXT  -- full JSON blob for drill-down
105	);
106	
107	CREATE INDEX idx_runs_project ON runs(project);
108	CREATE INDEX idx_runs_timestamp ON runs(timestamp);
109	
110	-- Optional: auto-vacuum old runs
111	-- Keep last 1000 runs per project, prune on insert
112	```
113	
114	Configuration:
115	
116	```yaml
117	# In .smoke.yaml on the dashboard server
118	dashboard:
119	  enabled: true
120	  db_path: "./smoke-dashboard.db"
121	  max_runs_per_project: 1000
122	  retention_days: 30
123	```
124	
125	If `db_path` is empty or `:memory:`, falls back to in-memory (useful for testing).
126	
127	---
128	
129	## 4. Frontend: Embedded Static HTML + Vanilla JS
130	
131	**Recommendation: Single HTML file embedded via `embed.FS`, vanilla JS with fetch API.**
132	
133	Why no build step:
134	- cosmo-smoke has zero npm/node dependency. Adding a JS build pipeline would violate the project's "minimal deps" design principle.
135	- The dashboard is a status board, not a web app. It needs: a table of projects with pass/fail status, click-through to test details, and a timestamp. Vanilla JS handles this in ~300 lines.
136	- `embed.FS` (Go 1.16+) compiles static files into the binary. Single binary deployment preserved.
137	
138	Structure:
139	
140	```
141	internal/dashboard/
142	├── handler.go          # HTTP handlers for API routes + dashboard
143	├── store.go            # SQLite storage layer
144	├── templates/
145	│   └── index.html      # Embedded HTML+CSS+JS (~400 lines)
146	```
147	
148	The HTML file contains inline CSS and JS. No external assets. Uses `fetch('/api/projects')` to populate the table, auto-refreshes every 30 seconds.
149	
150	Dashboard UI layout:
151	
152	```
153	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
154	│  CosmoLabs Smoke Dashboard          Last updated: 12:34 │
155	│  87/95 healthy  |  5 degraded  |  3 failing             │
156	├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
157	│  Project        | Tests | Pass | Fail | Last Run | Age  │
158	│  cosmo-api      |   12  |  12  |   0  | healthy  | 2m  │
159	│  cosmo-web      |    8  |   6  |   2  | failing  | 15m │
160	│  cosmo-auth     |    5  |   5  |   0  | healthy  | 5m  │
161	│  ...                                                    │
162	│  [click row for test details]                           │
163	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
164	```
165	
166	CSS: dark theme (terminal aesthetic matching cosmo-smoke's Lipgloss output), responsive, no framework.
167	
168	---
169	
170	## 5. API Design
171	
172	### `POST /api/results`
173	
174	Push endpoint. Receives the same JSON that `smoke run --format json` outputs.
175	
176	Request:
177	```json
178	{
179	  "project": "cosmo-api",
180	  "total": 12,
181	  "passed": 12,
182	  "failed": 0,
183	  "skipped": 0,
184	  "allowed_failures": 0,
185	  "duration_ms": 3400,
186	  "tests": [
187	    {"name": "health check", "passed": true, "duration_ms": 120, "assertions": [...]}
188	  ]
189	}
190	```
191	
192	Response: `202 Accepted` with `{"stored": true}`. Fire-and-forget from the client perspective.
193	
194	Validation: `project` field required, must be non-empty. If the project name is not in the allow-list (see Auth section), reject with `403`.
195	
196	### `GET /api/projects`
197	
198	```json
199	{
200	  "projects": [
201	    {
202	      "name": "cosmo-api",
203	      "latest_status": "healthy",
204	      "total_tests": 12,
205	      "passed": 12,
206	      "failed": 0,
207	      "last_run": "2026-04-19T12:34:56Z",
208	      "last_run_age_seconds": 120
209	    }
210	  ],
211	  "summary": {
212	    "total_projects": 95,
213	    "healthy": 87,
214	    "degraded": 5,
215	    "failing": 3
216	  }
217	}
218	```
219	
220	Query params: `?status=healthy|failing|degraded` to filter.
221	
222	### `GET /api/projects/{name}/history`
223	
224	```json
225	{
226	  "project": "cosmo-api",
227	  "runs": [
228	    {
229	      "timestamp": "2026-04-19T12:34:56Z",
230	      "total": 12,
231	      "passed": 12,
232	      "failed": 0,
233	      "duration_ms": 3400,
234	      "tests": [
235	        {"name": "health check", "passed": true, "duration_ms": 120}
236	      ]
237	    }
238	  ]
239	}
240	```
241	
242	Query params: `?limit=50` (default 50), `?since=2026-04-19T00:00:00Z`.
243	
244	### `GET /dashboard`
245	
246	Serves the embedded `index.html`. The JS fetches `/api/projects` and renders.
247	
248	---
249	
250	## 6. Auth: Portfolio-wide API Key
251	
252	**Recommendation: Single shared API key, header-based. No per-project tokens.**
253	
254	Why: Internal network. 95 projects pushing to one endpoint. Per-project token management is operational overhead for zero security gain on an internal network.
255	
256	Implementation:
257	
258	```bash
259	# Dashboard server side
260	smoke serve --dashboard --api-key "cosmo-internal-2026"
261	
262	# CI side
263	smoke run --report-url https://dashboard:8080/api/results --report-api-key "cosmo-internal-2026"
264	```
265	
266	The API key is validated on `POST /api/results` via `X-API-Key` header. `GET` endpoints (dashboard, API) are unauthenticated on the internal network.
267	
268	If `--api-key` is not set, all endpoints are open (default for dev/internal). This keeps the zero-config experience for local development.
269	
270	Key rotation: change the key in CI secrets and restart the dashboard. For 95 projects using a shared CI secret, this is a one-place change.
271	
272	---
273	
274	## 7. Implementation Plan
275	
276	### File Structure
277	
278	```
279	internal/dashboard/
280	├── dashboard.go        # Config, types, registration
281	├── handler.go          # HTTP handlers (POST /api/results, GET /api/projects, etc.)
282	├── store.go            # SQLite storage (insert, query, prune)
283	├── store_test.go       # Storage layer tests
284	├── templates/
285	│   └── index.html      # Embedded dashboard UI
286	
287	internal/reporter/
288	├── push.go             # PushReporter (new)
289	├── push_test.go        # PushReporter tests
290	
291	cmd/
292	├── serve.go            # Extended with --dashboard flag
293	├── run.go              # Extended with --report-url flag
294	```
295	
296	### Implementation Order
297	
298	**Phase 1: Push infrastructure (reporter side)**
299	
300	1. Create `internal/reporter/push.go` -- `PushReporter` that POSTs JSON to a URL on `Summary()`
301	2. Add `--report-url` and `--report-api-key` flags to `cmd/run.go`
302	3. Wire `PushReporter` in `runSmoke()` alongside existing reporters
303	4. Test: `smoke run --report-url http://localhost:8080/api/results` with a netcat listener
304	
305	**Phase 2: Storage layer**
306	
307	5. Add `modernc.org/sqlite` dependency
308	6. Create `internal/dashboard/store.go` -- SQLite schema, `InsertRun()`, `GetLatestByProject()`, `GetProjectHistory()`, `PruneOld()`
309	7. Create `internal/dashboard/store_test.go` -- full coverage with in-memory SQLite
310	
311	**Phase 3: API handlers**
312	
313	8. Create `internal/dashboard/handler.go` -- `POST /api/results`, `GET /api/projects`, `GET /api/projects/{name}/history`
314	9. Create `internal/dashboard/dashboard.go` -- config types, `RegisterRoutes(mux, store)`
315	10. Extend `cmd/serve.go` -- `--dashboard` flag, `--api-key`, `--db-path`, route registration
316	
317	**Phase 4: Frontend**
318	
319	11. Create `internal/dashboard/templates/index.html` -- dashboard UI
320	12. Add `GET /dashboard` handler serving embedded HTML
321	13. Auto-refresh via `setInterval(fetch, 30000)`
322	
323	**Phase 5: Polish**
324	
325	14. Graceful degradation: if SQLite fails, fall back to in-memory
326	15. Auto-prune on insert (keep last N per project)
327	16. README update for `smoke serve --dashboard`
328	
329	### Dependency Addition
330	
331	Only one new dependency: `modernc.org/sqline` (pure Go SQLite). Everything else is stdlib (`net/http`, `embed`, `database/sql`, `encoding/json`).
332	
333	### Estimated Size
334	
335	| File | Lines |
336	|------|-------|
337	| `internal/reporter/push.go` | ~80 |
338	| `internal/dashboard/store.go` | ~150 |
339	| `internal/dashboard/handler.go` | ~120 |
340	| `internal/dashboard/dashboard.go` | ~40 |
341	| `internal/dashboard/templates/index.html` | ~350 |
342	| Changes to `cmd/run.go` | ~20 |
343	| Changes to `cmd/serve.go` | ~30 |
344	| Tests | ~250 |
345	| **Total** | **~1040** |
346	
347	---
348	
349	## Open Questions
350	
351	1. **Project name source**: Should the `project` field in push payloads come from `.smoke.yaml`'s `project:` field (already exists in schema), or from a CLI flag? Recommendation: use the YAML field, with `--report-project` override for edge cases.
352	
353	2. **Stale project detection**: After how long should a project be marked "stale" (no report received)? Default proposal: 24 hours. Configurable via `dashboard.stale_after` in the server's `.smoke.yaml`.
354	
355	3. **CI pipeline template**: Should cosmo-smoke ship a GitHub Actions reusable workflow that includes the `--report-url` flag? That would make adoption across 95 projects a one-line include. Out of scope for v1 but worth noting.
356	
357	4. **WebSocket live updates**: The dashboard could use WebSocket to push updates instead of polling. Adds complexity to both sides. Proposal: v1 uses polling (30s interval). v2 can add WebSocket if the polling UX is insufficient.
358

# Portfolio Smoke Dashboard (ROAD-039)

**Date**: 2026-04-19
**Status**: Brainstorm
**Source**: IDEA-MO1FBRNZ, promoted to ROAD-039

## Goal

Central dashboard that aggregates `smoke run` results across CosmoLabs' ~95 projects for real-time portfolio health visibility. Single binary, zero external deps, runs on internal infra.

---

## 1. Backend Architecture: Extend `smoke serve`

**Recommendation: Extend `smoke serve` in-place. No separate binary.**

Why not a separate binary:
- The `smoke serve` command already has HTTP scaffolding (Cobra registration, `http.Server`, graceful shutdown, config loading). It is 158 lines and does one thing: run tests on `/healthz` and return JSON. Extending it is straightforward.
- A separate `smoke-dashboard` binary means a second build target, a second release pipeline, and two binaries to deploy. For internal tooling across 95 projects, one binary is simpler.
- The dashboard is fundamentally "serve HTTP with smoke data" -- it is the same domain as `smoke serve`.

How `smoke serve` grows:

```
smoke serve --port 8080 --dashboard
```

The `--dashboard` flag activates the aggregation mode. Without it, `smoke serve` behaves exactly as today (health-only). With it, the server gains these routes:

| Route | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `GET /healthz` | Existing behavior, unchanged |
| `POST /api/results` | Push endpoint for project reporters |
| `GET /api/projects` | List all projects with latest status |
| `GET /api/projects/{name}/history` | Run history for a project |
| `GET /dashboard` | HTML dashboard (embedded) |

The health-only mode keeps working for projects that just need a kube probe. The dashboard mode is opt-in.

---

## 2. Data Collection Model: Push

**Recommendation: Push model via `--report-url` flag on `smoke run`.**

Why push over pull for 95 projects:

- **Pull requires service discovery.** The dashboard would need to know the address of every project's `smoke serve` endpoint. That means a config file listing 95 URLs, or DNS-based discovery, or a service registry. All operational overhead.
- **Projects already run `smoke run` in CI.** Every CosmoLabs project runs smoke tests in CI pipelines. Adding `--report-url https://dashboard.internal:8080/api/results` to those CI jobs is a one-line change. The data flows exactly where it needs to go.
- **No agent required.** Pull means each project must run `smoke serve` as a long-lived process. Most projects don't -- they run `smoke run` and exit. Push matches the existing execution model.
- **CI is the natural source of truth.** Smoke results come from CI. CI pushes them to the dashboard. No additional infrastructure per project.

Implementation:

```bash
# In CI (each project's pipeline)
smoke run --format json --report-url https://smoke-dashboard.internal:8080/api/results
```

The `--report-url` flag causes `smoke run` to POST its JSON output to the URL after the run completes. This is a fire-and-forget HTTP POST in the existing `Summary()` path, identical to how `OTelReporter` already exports telemetry. The payload is exactly the same JSON that `--format json` produces today (`jsonOutput` struct).

Add a `PushReporter` alongside `OTelReporter` in `internal/reporter/`:

```go
type PushReporter struct {
    endpoint  string
    headers   map[string]string
    client    *http.Client
    // accumulates results, POSTs on Summary()
}
```

The `--report-url` flag wires it in via `withPushReport()` in `cmd/run.go`, same pattern as `withOTelExport()`.

---

## 3. Storage: SQLite

**Recommendation: SQLite via `modernc.org/sqlite` (pure Go, no CGO).**

Why not in-memory ring buffer:
- A ring buffer loses all data on restart. For a dashboard tracking portfolio health, historical trends matter. "Last 24 hours of CI" is the minimum useful window.
- Ring buffers also require manual compaction logic. SQLite handles this natively.

Why SQLite over Postgres/MySQL:
- Single binary deployment. No database server to run, connect, or configure.
- The write pattern is append-only (one POST per CI run, ~95 projects, maybe 10-20 runs/day each = ~2000 writes/day). SQLite handles this trivially.
- Read pattern is simple range queries (last N runs for project X). SQLite is excellent at this.
- `modernc.org/sqlite` is pure Go -- no CGO, cross-compile works, matches cosmo-smoke's "minimal deps" philosophy.

Schema:

```sql
CREATE TABLE runs (
    id          INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    project     TEXT NOT NULL,
    timestamp   DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    total       INTEGER,
    passed      INTEGER,
    failed      INTEGER,
    skipped     INTEGER,
    allowed_failures INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    duration_ms INTEGER,
    payload     TEXT  -- full JSON blob for drill-down
);

CREATE INDEX idx_runs_project ON runs(project);
CREATE INDEX idx_runs_timestamp ON runs(timestamp);

-- Optional: auto-vacuum old runs
-- Keep last 1000 runs per project, prune on insert
```

Configuration:

```yaml
# In .smoke.yaml on the dashboard server
dashboard:
  enabled: true
  db_path: "./smoke-dashboard.db"
  max_runs_per_project: 1000
  retention_days: 30
```

If `db_path` is empty or `:memory:`, falls back to in-memory (useful for testing).

---

## 4. Frontend: Embedded Static HTML + Vanilla JS

**Recommendation: Single HTML file embedded via `embed.FS`, vanilla JS with fetch API.**

Why no build step:
- cosmo-smoke has zero npm/node dependency. Adding a JS build pipeline would violate the project's "minimal deps" design principle.
- The dashboard is a status board, not a web app. It needs: a table of projects with pass/fail status, click-through to test details, and a timestamp. Vanilla JS handles this in ~300 lines.
- `embed.FS` (Go 1.16+) compiles static files into the binary. Single binary deployment preserved.

Structure:

```
internal/dashboard/
├── handler.go          # HTTP handlers for API routes + dashboard
├── store.go            # SQLite storage layer
├── templates/
│   └── index.html      # Embedded HTML+CSS+JS (~400 lines)
```

The HTML file contains inline CSS and JS. No external assets. Uses `fetch('/api/projects')` to populate the table, auto-refreshes every 30 seconds.

Dashboard UI layout:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CosmoLabs Smoke Dashboard          Last updated: 12:34 │
│  87/95 healthy  |  5 degraded  |  3 failing             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Project        | Tests | Pass | Fail | Last Run | Age  │
│  cosmo-api      |   12  |  12  |   0  | healthy  | 2m  │
│  cosmo-web      |    8  |   6  |   2  | failing  | 15m │
│  cosmo-auth     |    5  |   5  |   0  | healthy  | 5m  │
│  ...                                                    │
│  [click row for test details]                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

CSS: dark theme (terminal aesthetic matching cosmo-smoke's Lipgloss output), responsive, no framework.

---

## 5. API Design

### `POST /api/results`

Push endpoint. Receives the same JSON that `smoke run --format json` outputs.

Request:
```json
{
  "project": "cosmo-api",
  "total": 12,
  "passed": 12,
  "failed": 0,
  "skipped": 0,
  "allowed_failures": 0,
  "duration_ms": 3400,
  "tests": [
    {"name": "health check", "passed": true, "duration_ms": 120, "assertions": [...]}
  ]
}
```

Response: `202 Accepted` with `{"stored": true}`. Fire-and-forget from the client perspective.

Validation: `project` field required, must be non-empty. If the project name is not in the allow-list (see Auth section), reject with `403`.

### `GET /api/projects`

```json
{
  "projects": [
    {
      "name": "cosmo-api",
      "latest_status": "healthy",
      "total_tests": 12,
      "passed": 12,
      "failed": 0,
      "last_run": "2026-04-19T12:34:56Z",
      "last_run_age_seconds": 120
    }
  ],
  "summary": {
    "total_projects": 95,
    "healthy": 87,
    "degraded": 5,
    "failing": 3
  }
}
```

Query params: `?status=healthy|failing|degraded` to filter.

### `GET /api/projects/{name}/history`

```json
{
  "project": "cosmo-api",
  "runs": [
    {
      "timestamp": "2026-04-19T12:34:56Z",
      "total": 12,
      "passed": 12,
      "failed": 0,
      "duration_ms": 3400,
      "tests": [
        {"name": "health check", "passed": true, "duration_ms": 120}
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

Query params: `?limit=50` (default 50), `?since=2026-04-19T00:00:00Z`.

### `GET /dashboard`

Serves the embedded `index.html`. The JS fetches `/api/projects` and renders.

---

## 6. Auth: Portfolio-wide API Key

**Recommendation: Single shared API key, header-based. No per-project tokens.**

Why: Internal network. 95 projects pushing to one endpoint. Per-project token management is operational overhead for zero security gain on an internal network.

Implementation:

```bash
# Dashboard server side
smoke serve --dashboard --api-key "cosmo-internal-2026"

# CI side
smoke run --report-url https://dashboard:8080/api/results --report-api-key "cosmo-internal-2026"
```

The API key is validated on `POST /api/results` via `X-API-Key` header. `GET` endpoints (dashboard, API) are unauthenticated on the internal network.

If `--api-key` is not set, all endpoints are open (default for dev/internal). This keeps the zero-config experience for local development.

Key rotation: change the key in CI secrets and restart the dashboard. For 95 projects using a shared CI secret, this is a one-place change.

---

## 7. Implementation Plan

### File Structure

```
internal/dashboard/
├── dashboard.go        # Config, types, registration
├── handler.go          # HTTP handlers (POST /api/results, GET /api/projects, etc.)
├── store.go            # SQLite storage (insert, query, prune)
├── store_test.go       # Storage layer tests
├── templates/
│   └── index.html      # Embedded dashboard UI

internal/reporter/
├── push.go             # PushReporter (new)
├── push_test.go        # PushReporter tests

cmd/
├── serve.go            # Extended with --dashboard flag
├── run.go              # Extended with --report-url flag
```

### Implementation Order

**Phase 1: Push infrastructure (reporter side)**

1. Create `internal/reporter/push.go` -- `PushReporter` that POSTs JSON to a URL on `Summary()`
2. Add `--report-url` and `--report-api-key` flags to `cmd/run.go`
3. Wire `PushReporter` in `runSmoke()` alongside existing reporters
4. Test: `smoke run --report-url http://localhost:8080/api/results` with a netcat listener

**Phase 2: Storage layer**

5. Add `modernc.org/sqlite` dependency
6. Create `internal/dashboard/store.go` -- SQLite schema, `InsertRun()`, `GetLatestByProject()`, `GetProjectHistory()`, `PruneOld()`
7. Create `internal/dashboard/store_test.go` -- full coverage with in-memory SQLite

**Phase 3: API handlers**

8. Create `internal/dashboard/handler.go` -- `POST /api/results`, `GET /api/projects`, `GET /api/projects/{name}/history`
9. Create `internal/dashboard/dashboard.go` -- config types, `RegisterRoutes(mux, store)`
10. Extend `cmd/serve.go` -- `--dashboard` flag, `--api-key`, `--db-path`, route registration

**Phase 4: Frontend**

11. Create `internal/dashboard/templates/index.html` -- dashboard UI
12. Add `GET /dashboard` handler serving embedded HTML
13. Auto-refresh via `setInterval(fetch, 30000)`

**Phase 5: Polish**

14. Graceful degradation: if SQLite fails, fall back to in-memory
15. Auto-prune on insert (keep last N per project)
16. README update for `smoke serve --dashboard`

### Dependency Addition

Only one new dependency: `modernc.org/sqline` (pure Go SQLite). Everything else is stdlib (`net/http`, `embed`, `database/sql`, `encoding/json`).

### Estimated Size

| File | Lines |
|------|-------|
| `internal/reporter/push.go` | ~80 |
| `internal/dashboard/store.go` | ~150 |
| `internal/dashboard/handler.go` | ~120 |
| `internal/dashboard/dashboard.go` | ~40 |
| `internal/dashboard/templates/index.html` | ~350 |
| Changes to `cmd/run.go` | ~20 |
| Changes to `cmd/serve.go` | ~30 |
| Tests | ~250 |
| **Total** | **~1040** |

---

## Open Questions

1. **Project name source**: Should the `project` field in push payloads come from `.smoke.yaml`'s `project:` field (already exists in schema), or from a CLI flag? Recommendation: use the YAML field, with `--report-project` override for edge cases.

2. **Stale project detection**: After how long should a project be marked "stale" (no report received)? Default proposal: 24 hours. Configurable via `dashboard.stale_after` in the server's `.smoke.yaml`.

3. **CI pipeline template**: Should cosmo-smoke ship a GitHub Actions reusable workflow that includes the `--report-url` flag? That would make adoption across 95 projects a one-line include. Out of scope for v1 but worth noting.

4. **WebSocket live updates**: The dashboard could use WebSocket to push updates instead of polling. Adds complexity to both sides. Proposal: v1 uses polling (30s interval). v2 can add WebSocket if the polling UX is insufficient.

1	package reporter
2	
3	import "time"
4	
5	// Reporter receives events during test execution.
6	type Reporter interface {
7		PrereqStart(name string)
8		PrereqResult(r PrereqResultData)
9		TestStart(name string)
10		TestResult(r TestResultData)
11		Summary(s SuiteResultData)
12	}
13	
14	// PrereqResultData holds the outcome of a prerequisite check.
15	type PrereqResultData struct {
16		Name   string
17		Passed bool
18		Output string
19		Hint   string
20		Error  error
21	}
22	
23	// AssertionDetail holds one assertion's outcome.
24	type AssertionDetail struct {
25		Type     string `json:"type"`
26		Expected string `json:"expected"`
27		Actual   string `json:"actual"`
28		Passed   bool   `json:"passed"`
29	}
30	
31	// TestResultData holds the outcome of a single test.
32	type TestResultData struct {
33		Name           string            `json:"name"`
34		Passed         bool              `json:"passed"`
35		Skipped        bool              `json:"skipped"`
36		AllowedFailure bool              `json:"allowed_failure,omitempty"`
37		Duration       time.Duration     `json:"duration"`
38		Assertions     []AssertionDetail `json:"assertions"`
39		Error          error             `json:"-"`
40	}
41	
42	// SuiteResultData holds the aggregate results.
43	type SuiteResultData struct {
44		Project         string           `json:"project"`
45		Total           int              `json:"total"`
46		Passed          int              `json:"passed"`
47		Failed          int              `json:"failed"`
48		Skipped         int              `json:"skipped"`
49		AllowedFailures int              `json:"allowed_failures"`
50		Duration        time.Duration    `json:"duration"`
51		Tests           []TestResultData `json:"tests"`
52		TraceHealthPct  float64          `json:"trace_health_pct,omitempty"`
53		TraceDegraded   bool             `json:"trace_degraded,omitempty"`
54	}
55

1	package cmd
2	
3	import (
4		"context"
5		"encoding/json"
6		"fmt"
7		"net/http"
8		"os"
9		"os/signal"
10		"path/filepath"
11		"syscall"
12		"time"
13	
14		"github.com/CosmoLabs-org/cosmo-smoke/internal/reporter"
15		"github.com/CosmoLabs-org/cosmo-smoke/internal/runner"
16		"github.com/CosmoLabs-org/cosmo-smoke/internal/schema"
17		"github.com/spf13/cobra"
18	)
19	
20	var serveCmd = &cobra.Command{
21		Use:   "serve",
22		Short: "Start HTTP health endpoint for container probes",
23		Long:  "Expose a /healthz endpoint that runs smoke tests on each request",
24		RunE:  runServe,
25	}
26	
27	var (
28		servePort       string
29		servePath       string
30		serveConfigFile string
31	)
32	
33	func init() {
34		rootCmd.AddCommand(serveCmd)
35		serveCmd.Flags().StringVarP(&servePort, "port", "p", "8080", "Port to listen on")
36		serveCmd.Flags().StringVar(&servePath, "path", "/healthz", "Health endpoint path")
37		serveCmd.Flags().StringVarP(&serveConfigFile, "file", "f", ".smoke.yaml", "Config file path")
38	}
39	
40	// noopReporter satisfies reporter.Reporter without emitting any output.
41	type noopReporter struct{}
42	
43	func newNoopReporter() *noopReporter { return &noopReporter{} }
44	
45	func (n *noopReporter) PrereqStart(_ string)                              {}
46	func (n *noopReporter) PrereqResult(_ reporter.PrereqResultData)          {}
47	func (n *noopReporter) TestStart(_ string)                                {}
48	func (n *noopReporter) TestResult(_ reporter.TestResultData)              {}
49	func (n *noopReporter) Summary(_ reporter.SuiteResultData)                {}
50	
51	// healthResponse is the JSON body returned by the health endpoint.
52	type healthResponse struct {
53		Status     string      `json:"status"`
54		Tests      testCounts  `json:"tests"`
55		DurationMs int64       `json:"duration_ms"`
56	}
57	
58	type testCounts struct {
59		Total  int `json:"total"`
60		Passed int `json:"passed"`
61		Failed int `json:"failed"`
62	}
63	
64	// buildHandler returns an http.HandlerFunc that runs smoke tests on every
65	// request and responds with the appropriate status and JSON body.
66	func buildHandler(configFile string) http.HandlerFunc {
67		return func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
68			start := time.Now()
69	
70			cfg, err := schema.Load(configFile)
71			if err != nil {
72				writeHealthError(w, http.StatusInternalServerError, fmt.Sprintf("load config: %v", err))
73				return
74			}
75			if err := schema.Validate(cfg); err != nil {
76				writeHealthError(w, http.StatusInternalServerError, fmt.Sprintf("invalid config: %v", err))
77				return
78			}
79	
80			configDir := filepath.Dir(configFile)
81			if !filepath.IsAbs(configDir) {
82				cwd, _ := os.Getwd()
83				configDir = filepath.Join(cwd, configDir)
84			}
85	
86			noop := newNoopReporter()
87			rn := &runner.Runner{
88				Config:    cfg,
89				Reporter:  noop,
90				ConfigDir: configDir,
91			}
92	
93			result, err := rn.Run(runner.RunOptions{})
94			elapsed := time.Since(start)
95			if err != nil {
96				writeHealthError(w, http.StatusInternalServerError, fmt.Sprintf("run: %v", err))
97				return
98			}
99	
100			status := "healthy"
101			httpStatus := http.StatusOK
102			if result.Failed > 0 {
103				status = "unhealthy"
104				httpStatus = http.StatusServiceUnavailable
105			}
106	
107			resp := healthResponse{
108				Status: status,
109				Tests: testCounts{
110					Total:  result.Total,
111					Passed: result.Passed,
112					Failed: result.Failed,
113				},
114				DurationMs: elapsed.Milliseconds(),
115			}
116	
117			w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
118			w.WriteHeader(httpStatus)
119			enc := json.NewEncoder(w)
120			enc.SetIndent("", "  ")
121			enc.Encode(resp) //nolint:errcheck
122		}
123	}
124	
125	func writeHealthError(w http.ResponseWriter, code int, msg string) {
126		w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
127		w.WriteHeader(code)
128		json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(map[string]string{"error": msg}) //nolint:errcheck
129	}
130	
131	func runServe(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
132		addr := ":" + servePort
133	
134		mux := http.NewServeMux()
135		mux.HandleFunc(servePath, buildHandler(serveConfigFile))
136	
137		srv := &http.Server{
138			Addr:    addr,
139			Handler: mux,
140		}
141	
142		// Graceful shutdown on SIGINT/SIGTERM.
143		quit := make(chan os.Signal, 1)
144		signal.Notify(quit, syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM)
145	
146		go func() {
147			<-quit
148			ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 5*time.Second)
149			defer cancel()
150			srv.Shutdown(ctx) //nolint:errcheck
151		}()
152	
153		fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "smoke serve listening on %s%s\n", addr, servePath)
154		if err := srv.ListenAndServe(); err != nil && err != http.ErrServerClosed {
155			return err
156		}
157		return nil
158	}

25	// NewOTelReporter creates a reporter that exports spans to the given OTLP HTTP endpoint.
26	func NewOTelReporter(endpoint, service string, headers map[string]string) *OTelReporter {
27		var tid [16]byte
28		rand.Read(tid[:])
29		return &OTelReporter{
30			endpoint: endpoint,
31			service:  service,
32			headers:  headers,
33			traceID:  hex.EncodeToString(tid[:]),
34			client:   &http.Client{Timeout: 5 * time.Second},
35		}
36	}

file.content:
package cmd

import (
	"fmt"
	"net/url"
	"os"
	"os/signal"
	"path/filepath"
	"syscall"
	"time"

	"github.com/CosmoLabs-org/cosmo-smoke/internal/monorepo"
	"github.com/CosmoLabs-org/cosmo-smoke/internal/reporter"
	"github.com/CosmoLabs-org/cosmo-smoke/internal/runner"
	"github.com/CosmoLabs-org/cosmo-smoke/internal/schema"
	"github.com/fsnotify/fsnotify"
	"github.com/spf13/cobra"
)

// withOTelExport wraps the reporter with OTel telemetry export if configured.
func withOTelExport(rep reporter.Reporter, cfg *schema.SmokeConfig) reporter.Reporter {
	if !cfg.OTel.Enabled {
		return rep
	}
	exportURL := cfg.OTel.ExportURL
	if exportURL == "" && cfg.OTel.JaegerURL != "" {
		exportURL = cfg.OTel.JaegerURL + "/v1/traces"
	}
	if exportURL == "" {
		return rep
	}
	if _, err := url.Parse(exportURL); err != nil {
		fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "warning: invalid otel export url: %v\n", err)
		return rep
	}
	service := cfg.OTel.ServiceName
	if service == "" {
		service = cfg.Project
	}
	otel := reporter.NewOTelReporter(exportURL, service, cfg.OTel.ExportHeaders)
	return reporter.NewMultiReporter(rep, otel)
}

var runCmd = &cobra.Command{
	Use:   "run",
	Short: "Run smoke tests",
	Long:  "Execute smoke tests defined in .smoke.yaml",
	RunE:  runSmoke,
}

var (
	configFile   string
	tags         []string
	excludeTags  []string
	format       string
	failFast     bool
	timeout      string
	dryRun       bool
	watch        bool
	envName      string
	monorepoMode bool
	otelCollector string
	noOtel       bool
)

func init() {
	rootCmd.AddCommand(runCmd)
	runCmd.Flags().StringVarP(&configFile, "file", "f", ".smoke.yaml", "Config file path")
	runCmd.Flags().StringSliceVar(&tags, "tag", nil, "Include only tests with these tags")
	runCmd.Flags().StringSliceVar(&excludeTags, "exclude-tag", nil, "Exclude tests with these tags")
	runCmd.Flags().StringVar(&format, "format", "terminal", "Output format (terminal|json|junit|tap|prometheus)")
	runCmd.Flags().BoolVar(&failFast, "fail-fast", false, "Stop on first failure")
	runCmd.Flags().StringVar(&timeout, "timeout", "", "Per-test timeout override (e.g. 30s)")
	runCmd.Flags().BoolVar(&dryRun, "dry-run", false, "List tests without running")
	runCmd.Flags().BoolVar(&watch, "watch", false, "Re-run tests when files change (Ctrl+C to exit)")
	runCmd.Flags().StringVar(&envName, "env", "", "Load environment-specific config (e.g. staging loads staging.smoke.yaml)")
	runCmd.Flags().BoolVar(&monorepoMode, "monorepo", false, "Auto-discover .smoke.yaml in subdirectories")
	runCmd.Flags().StringVar(&otelCollector, "otel-collector", "", "Override otel.jaeger_url and enable tracing")
	runCmd.Flags().BoolVar(&noOtel, "no-otel", false, "Disable otel trace propagation for this run")
}

content.description: Phase 5: Graceful degradation (SQLite → in-memory fallback), auto-prune on insert, CLAUDE.md update, README for smoke serve --dashboard, full test suite run.

content.subject: ROAD-039 Phase 1: PushReporter + --report-url flag
content.description: Phase 1: Create internal/reporter/push.go — PushReporter that POSTs JSON to a URL on Summary(). Add --report-url and --report-api-key flags to cmd/run.go. Wire PushReporter in runSmoke(). Test with netcat.

content.subject: ROAD-039 Phase 3: API handlers + serve integration
content.description: Phase 3: Create internal/dashboard/handler.go with POST /api/results, GET /api/projects, GET /api/projects/{name}/history. Create dashboard.go with config types. Extend cmd/serve.go with --dashboard, --api-key, --db-path flags.

content.subject: ROAD-039 Phase 4: Embedded frontend
content.description: Phase 4: Create internal/dashboard/templates/index.html — embedded dashboard UI with vanilla JS, dark theme, auto-refresh. Add GET /dashboard handler serving embedded HTML via embed.FS.

content.subject: ROAD-039 Phase 2: SQLite storage layer
content.description: Phase 2: Add modernc.org/sqlite dep. Create internal/dashboard/store.go with SQLite schema, InsertRun(), GetLatestByProject(), GetProjectHistory(), PruneOld(). Create store_test.go with in-memory coverage.

file.content:
go build -ldflags "-s -w -X github.com/CosmoLabs-org/cosmo-smoke/cmd.Version=X.Y.Z" -o smoke .
```

## Commands

```bash
smoke run [--tag X] [--exclude-tag X] [--format terminal|json|junit|tap|prometheus] [--fail-fast] [--timeout 30s] [-f path] [--dry-run] [--watch] [--monorepo] [--otel-collector URL] [--no-otel]
smoke init [--force] [--from-running CONTAINER]
smoke version
```

## Assertion Types

| Type | Field | Description |
|------|-------|-------------|
| exit_code | `*int` | Process exit code match |
| stdout_contains | `string` | Substring match on stdout |
| stdout_matches | `string` | Regex match on stdout |
| stderr_contains | `string` | Substring match on stderr |
| stderr_matches | `string` | Regex match on stderr |

smoke init [--force] [--from-running CONTAINER]

# cosmo-smoke — Project Instructions

## Overview

Universal smoke test runner. Standalone Go binary that reads `.smoke.yaml` and runs lightweight smoke tests. Designed for CosmoLabs' ~95-project portfolio.

**Repository**: `github.com/CosmoLabs-org/cosmo-smoke`
**Company**: CosmoLabs
**Version**: 0.8.0

## Architecture

```
cmd/
├── root.go          # Cobra root command with banner
├── run.go           # smoke run — main entry point
├── init_cmd.go      # smoke init — auto-detect + generate config
└── version.go       # smoke version (ldflags-injected)
internal/
├── schema/          # SmokeConfig structs, YAML parsing, validation
├── runner/          # Assertion engine (29 types), prereq runner, test execution
├── reporter/        # Terminal (Lipgloss) + JSON reporters
├── monorepo/        # Sub-config discovery for monorepo projects
└── detector/        # Project type detection + template generation
```

## Key Design Decisions

- **Minimal deps**: Cobra + Lipgloss + yaml.v3 + gjson. No Viper, no Bubbletea.
- **Pure assertions**: All 29 assertion types are pure functions — no side effects.
- **Config inheritance**: `includes:` directive + Go templates (`{{ .Env.FOO }}`).
- **Config-dir-relative**: Commands execute from the config file's directory, not cwd.
- **All errors at once**: Validation returns all errors, not just the first.
- **Reporter interface**: Terminal and JSON reporters are pluggable via interface.
- **Watch mode**: `--watch` keeps smoke resident and re-runs on file changes. fsnotify-backed. 500ms debounce. When OTel is enabled, tracks trace health across runs with a sliding window (last 10 runs). Alerts when health drops below 50%.
- **Retry**: Opt-in `retry: {count, backoff, retry_on_trace_only?}` on test level. Exponential backoff. No side effects on pass-first-try. `retry_on_trace_only` skips retry when the otel_trace assertion confirms the trace was received.
- **Monorepo**: `--monorepo` flag auto-discovers `.smoke.yaml` in subdirectories. Unlimited depth, configurable exclusions.
- **WebSocket**: Stdlib-only WebSocket client. Connect-send-expect pattern with no external deps.
- **gRPC opt-in**: gRPC health check excluded from default build. Use `-tags grpc` to include.

## Build & Test

```bash
go build ./...                    # Build
go test ./...                     # Run all tests (314 total)
smoke run                         # Self-smoke (6 tests)
go build -ldflags "-s -w -X github.com/CosmoLabs-org/cosmo-smoke/cmd.Version=X.Y.Z" -o smoke .
```

## Commands

```bash
smoke run [--tag X] [--exclude-tag X] [--format terminal|json|junit|tap|prometheus] [--fail-fast] [--timeout 30s] [-f path] [--dry-run] [--watch] [--monorepo] [--otel-collector URL] [--no-otel]
smoke init [--force] [--from-running CONTAINER]
smoke version
```

## Assertion Types

| Type | Field | Description |
|------|-------|-------------|
| exit_code | `*int` | Process exit code match |
| stdout_contains | `string` | Substring match on stdout |
| stdout_matches | `string` | Regex match on stdout |
| stderr_contains | `string` | Substring match on stderr |
| stderr_matches | `string` | Regex match on stderr |
| file_exists | `string` | File exists relative to config dir |
| env_exists | `string` | Environment variable exists |
| port_listening | `{port, protocol?, host?}` | TCP/UDP port is open |
| process_running | `string` | Named process currently running (pgrep -x / tasklist) |
| http | `{url, method?, status_code?, body_contains?, body_matches?, header_contains?}` | HTTP endpoint check |
| json_field | `{path, equals?, contains?, matches?}` | JSONPath assertion on stdout |
| response_time_ms | `*int` | Test duration must not exceed this threshold |
| ssl_cert | `{host, port?, min_days_remaining?, allow_self_signed?}` | TLS cert valid + expiry threshold |
| redis_ping | `{host?, port?, password?}` | Redis PING returns +PONG (RESP protocol) |
| memcached_version | `{host?, port?}` | Memcached `version` command returns VERSION |
| postgres_ping | `{host?, port?}` | Postgres server SSLRequest handshake returns valid protocol byte |
| mysql_ping | `{host?, port?}` | MySQL server sends valid v10 handshake packet on connection |
| grpc_health | `{address, service?, use_tls?, timeout?}` | grpc.health.v1 Health/Check returns SERVING (requires `-tags grpc`) |
| websocket | `{url, send?, expect_contains?, expect_matches?, timeout?}` | WebSocket connect-send-expect assertion |
| docker_container_running | `{name}` | Named Docker container is currently running |
| docker_image_exists | `{image}` | Docker image exists locally |
| url_reachable | `{url, timeout?, status_code?}` | HTTP/HTTPS connectivity check |
| service_reachable | `{url, timeout?}` | External service dependency check |
| s3_bucket | `{bucket, region?, endpoint?}` | S3-compatible bucket accessibility (anonymous HEAD) |
| version_check | `{command, pattern}` | Tool version verification via shell command + regex |
| otel_trace | `{backend?, jaeger_url, service_name?, min_spans?, timeout?, api_key?, dd_app_key?}` | Trace verification with W3C traceparent propagation. Backends: jaeger (default), tempo, honeycomb, datadog |
| credential_check | `{source, name, contains?}` | Credential accessible without leaking value (env\|file\|exec) |
| graphql | `{url, query?, status_code?, expect_types?, expect_contains?, timeout?}` | GraphQL introspection assertion |

Plus `allow_failure: true` on Test for flaky/allowed-failure tests.

## OpenTelemetry Integration

```yaml
otel:
  enabled: true
  jaeger_url: "http://jaeger:16686"
  service_name: "cosmo-smoke"
  trace_propagation: true
```

When enabled, W3C `traceparent` headers are auto-injected into HTTP, gRPC, and WebSocket assertions. The `otel_trace` assertion verifies traces arrived at a collector (supports Jaeger, Tempo, Honeycomb, Datadog backends).

Smoke test results are also exported as OTLP telemetry when `export_url` is configured or `jaeger_url` is set (auto-appends `/v1/traces`). Each test becomes a span with attributes for pass/fail status, duration, and assertion details.

## Output Formats

`smoke run --format X` supports: `terminal` (default), `json`, `junit`, `tap`, `prometheus`.

## Detected Project Types

Go, Node (bun/npm), Python, Docker, Rust — each with tailored smoke test templates.

+smoke run [--tag X] [--exclude-tag X] [--format terminal|json|junit|tap|prometheus] [--fail-fast] [--timeout 30s] [-f path] [--dry-run] [--watch] [--monorepo] [--otel-collector URL] [--no-otel] [--report-url URL] [--report-api-key KEY]

Universal smoke test runner. Standalone Go binary that reads `.smoke.yaml` and runs lightweight smoke tests. Designed for CosmoLabs' ~95-project portfolio.
